items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how green roofs reduce urban stormwater runoff
items.transcript: Urban flooding often has less to do with how much rain falls than with how quickly that rain arrives at the drainage system. This is where green roofs come in. A green roof replaces a conventional impervious surface with a shallow layer of growing medium and low, hardy vegetation, planted above a waterproofing membrane and a drainage layer. Engineers distinguish between two broad types: extensive green roofs, which have a thin substrate and require little maintenance, and intensive green roofs, which carry a deeper soil layer and can support small trees or shrubs. Both types work on the same basic principle. Rainwater that lands on the roof is first intercepted by plant leaves, then absorbed by the substrate, where it's held until it evaporates or is taken up by roots. Only the excess eventually drains away, and it does so slowly. Chicago's City Hall, one of the earliest large-scale installations in North America, has been monitored closely since it was planted in 2001. Researchers there recorded that the roof retained roughly seventy percent of the rain that fell during typical summer storms, releasing the rest over several hours instead of within minutes. That delay is the whole point. Municipal sewer systems are sized to handle a certain flow rate, and a sudden pulse of runoff from thousands of conventional roofs can push combined sewers past capacity, sending untreated overflow into rivers. By spreading the same volume of water across a longer period, green roofs keep peak flows below that threshold. City planners increasingly treat vegetated roofs less as decoration and more as distributed infrastructure, quietly doing work that would otherwise fall entirely on pipes buried underground.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How green roofs slow down and reduce stormwater runoff in cities
options.B: Why Chicago City Hall installed a vegetated roof in 2001
options.C: How combined sewer systems are designed to handle heavy rain
options.D: The differences between extensive and intensive roof gardens

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 概括主旨：绿色屋顶如何减缓并减少城市雨水径流。B 只是讲座中提到的一个具体案例（芝加哥市政厅），属于 too_narrow。C 讲的是排水系统的设计，是相关但非本讲座重点的话题，属 wrong_topic。D 提到的粗放型与密集型屋顶的区别只是讲座中的一个细节，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to rainwater that lands on a green roof?

options.A: It is channeled immediately into underground storage tanks
options.B: It is absorbed by the substrate and released gradually
options.C: It flows off the roof faster than from a bare surface
options.D: It is filtered through pipes before reaching the plants

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 与讲座一致：基质吸收雨水并逐渐释放。A 说雨水被直接引入地下蓄水池，讲座未提及，属 wrong_detail。C 说绿色屋顶径流比裸露表面更快，与讲座所述相反，属 reversed_logic。D 说水经管道过滤后才到达植物，讲座未如此描述，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the distinction between extensive and intensive green roofs?

options.A: To argue that intensive roofs are always more effective than extensive ones
options.B: To explain how Chicago City Hall's roof was originally constructed
options.C: To show that different roof designs share the same basic principle
options.D: To restate that both roof types have substrate and vegetation

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 说明教授提及粗放型与密集型屋顶区别的目的：说明尽管设计不同，两者遵循相同的基本原理。A 说密集型屋顶总是更有效，讲座并未做此比较，属 over_inference。B 误将该区别的目的说成是描述芝加哥市政厅屋顶的建造方式，属 wrong_purpose。D 只是复述两种屋顶都有基质和植被这一事实，没有解释为何提及，属 literal_restatement。

questions.stem: Why does the professor bring up the flow capacity of combined sewer systems?

options.A: To describe how sewer pipes are physically constructed underground
options.B: To argue that cities should stop building sewer systems entirely
options.C: To restate that sewers are sized to handle a certain flow rate
options.D: To explain why slowing down peak runoff helps prevent overflow

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 说明教授提及下水道流量容量的目的：解释为何减缓径流高峰能够防止溢流。A 描述下水道管道的物理构造，与该处目的无关，属 wrong_purpose。B 说教授主张彻底取消下水道系统，是未表达的过度推断，属 over_inference。C 只是复述下水道按一定流量设计这一事实，没有说明提及的原因，属 literal_restatement。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: how invasive species disrupt native food webs
items.transcript: An invasive species is an organism introduced, deliberately or accidentally, into an ecosystem where it did not evolve, and where it causes ecological or economic harm. Not every non-native species qualifies; many introduced plants and animals settle into a new environment without doing much damage. What makes a species invasive is its capacity to spread rapidly and disrupt the relationships that already exist among native organisms. The lionfish offers a striking case study. Native to the Indo-Pacific, lionfish were first spotted off the coast of Florida in the 1980s, likely released from home aquariums, and within a few decades they had spread across the Caribbean and up the Atlantic seaboard. On coral reefs, lionfish are voracious generalist predators, and they consume juvenile fish of dozens of native species, including the small herbivores that normally graze algae off coral surfaces. Because Caribbean reef fish evolved without this predator, they show almost no avoidance behavior around it. Researchers diving on invaded reefs have documented native fish populations dropping by well over half within just a couple of years. When the algae-grazing fish disappear, algae can overgrow and smother coral, weakening the reef structure that supports the entire food web. So the damage isn't confined to the species lionfish eat directly. It cascades outward, altering competition, grazing pressure, and ultimately the physical habitat itself. Understanding these ripple effects is one reason marine biologists now encourage divers and fishers to actively remove lionfish wherever they're found, treating control efforts as a form of ecosystem triage rather than a lost cause.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How lionfish were first introduced into Florida's coastal waters
options.B: How invasive species like lionfish disrupt native food webs
options.C: Why coral reefs are especially vulnerable to algae overgrowth
options.D: The methods researchers use to count native fish populations

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 概括主旨：狮子鱼这样的入侵物种如何破坏本地食物网。A 只是讲座开头提到的引入历史细节，属 too_narrow。C 谈珊瑚礁为何易受藻类过度生长影响，是相关但非讲座重点的话题，属 wrong_topic。D 借用了“研究者”一词，但讲座并未讲述统计方法，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do Caribbean reef fish fail to avoid lionfish?

options.A: Lionfish are much faster swimmers than native reef predators
options.B: Lionfish hide inside coral crevices where native fish cannot see them
options.C: Native fish never evolved alongside this predator and don't recognize it
options.D: Native fish evolved specific defenses against this type of predator

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与讲座一致：本地鱼类从未与这种捕食者共同演化，因此不认得它是威胁。A 说狮子鱼游得更快，讲座未提及速度，属 wrong_detail。B 说狮子鱼躲在珊瑚缝隙中，讲座未如此描述，属 topic_adjacent。D 说本地鱼类演化出了针对性防御，与讲座所述“几乎没有躲避行为”相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What did researchers observe on reefs invaded by lionfish?

options.A: Native fish populations increased slightly despite the presence of lionfish
options.B: Lionfish primarily preyed on adult fish rather than juvenile fish
options.C: Algae-grazing fish became more numerous once lionfish arrived
options.D: Native fish populations fell by more than half within a few years

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 与讲座一致：本地鱼类数量在短短几年内下降超过一半。A 说本地鱼类数量反而增加，与事实相反，属 reversed_logic。B 说狮子鱼主要捕食成年鱼，但讲座说它们捕食幼鱼，属 wrong_detail。C 说食藻鱼数量增多，与讲座所述“食藻鱼消失”相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the effects of lionfish on coral reefs?

options.A: The damage extends beyond fish lionfish eat directly, reshaping the reef habitat
options.B: Coral reefs will fully recover once lionfish populations decline
options.C: Lionfish harm coral more directly than they harm native fish
options.D: Lionfish removal efforts have proven effective in every location tried

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 与讲座结尾呼应：破坏不局限于狮子鱼直接捕食的鱼类，而是波及整个礁栖息地。B 说珊瑚礁会完全恢复，讲座未支持这一乐观结论，属 over_generalization。C 说狮子鱼对珊瑚的伤害比对本地鱼类更直接，与讲座所述机制相反，属 reversed_logic。D 说清除行动在所有地点都被证明有效，讲座只说这是被鼓励尝试的方法，并未给出效果数据，属 topic_adjacent。

items.subject: architecture
items.topic: the influence of climate on traditional vernacular housing
items.transcript: Vernacular architecture refers to buildings designed not by trained architects following a blueprint, but by ordinary builders working within a local tradition, using materials found nearby and responding directly to the local climate. Long before mechanical heating and cooling existed, comfort depended entirely on how a house was built. Consider the adobe houses common across the American Southwest. Adobe walls are made of sun-dried mud brick, and they're often two feet thick or more. That thickness matters enormously in a hot desert climate. Thick mud walls absorb heat slowly during the day and release it slowly at night, which keeps interiors relatively cool when it's blazing outside and relatively warm after the sun goes down. Small, deep-set windows further limit the amount of direct sun entering the rooms. Now compare that with traditional houses built in regions that receive heavy snowfall, such as parts of Scandinavia or the Alps. There, roofs are built steeply pitched, sometimes at forty-five degrees or more. A steep roof lets snow slide off under its own weight instead of piling up and collapsing the structure. Roof overhangs extend far beyond the walls, keeping snowmelt away from the foundation. Neither design would work well in the other's climate. An adobe house in a snowy region would trap moisture and crack; a steep alpine roof serves no real purpose in a dry desert. What both traditions show is that generations of builders, without any formal engineering training, arrived at highly effective climate solutions through trial, error, and careful observation passed down over time.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The materials used to construct adobe houses in the Southwest
options.B: How modern architects design energy-efficient buildings today
options.C: How traditional houses were shaped by local climate conditions
options.D: The history of formal architectural training and blueprints

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 概括主旨：传统住宅如何受当地气候条件塑造。A 只是其中一个例子（西南部土坯房）用到的材料，属 too_narrow。B 谈现代建筑师设计节能建筑，讲座讲的是传统建筑而非现代，属 wrong_topic。D 借用了“正规建筑训练”一词，但讲座恰恰强调传统建造者并无正规训练，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the thickness of adobe walls?

options.A: To describe how mud bricks are manufactured and dried
options.B: To argue that adobe is a stronger material than wood
options.C: To explain why adobe houses are difficult to repair
options.D: To show how wall thickness helps regulate indoor temperature

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 说明教授提及土坯墙厚度的目的：展示墙体厚度如何帮助调节室内温度。A 只是描述土坯砖如何制作晾干，并非提及厚度的原因，属 literal_restatement。B 说土坯比木材更坚固，讲座未做此比较，属 over_inference。C 说土坯房难以维修，讲座未提及维修问题，属 wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare adobe houses with steep alpine roofs?

options.A: To show that each design solves the specific climate problem it faces
options.B: To argue that alpine roofs are more effective than adobe walls
options.C: To restate that adobe walls are two feet thick or more
options.D: To explain how snow accumulates on flat rooftops

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 说明教授对比土坯房与阿尔卑斯陡屋顶的目的：展示每种设计都针对各自面对的具体气候问题。B 说陡屋顶比土坯墙更有效，讲座并未做整体优劣比较，属 over_inference。C 只是复述土坯墙厚度这一事实，没有解释比较的原因，属 literal_restatement。D 讲平屋顶积雪的问题，但讲座讨论的是陡屋顶而非平屋顶，属 wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about traditional builders?

options.A: They relied on formal engineering training to design their houses
options.B: They developed effective solutions through experience rather than formal training
options.C: They borrowed most of their techniques from foreign architects
options.D: They built houses that work equally well in any climate

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 与讲座结尾呼应：几代建造者没有正规工程训练，却通过反复试验、犯错和观察得出了有效的气候解决方案。A 说他们依靠正规工程训练，与讲座所述相反，属 reversed_logic。C 说技术多借自外国建筑师，讲座未提及，属 topic_adjacent。D 说这些房屋在任何气候下都同样适用，讲座明确指出两种设计互不适用对方气候，属 over_generalization。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how gift-giving rituals reinforce social bonds across cultures
items.transcript: Gift-giving might seem like a simple act of generosity, but anthropologists have long argued that in many societies it functions as a binding social contract. The French sociologist Marcel Mauss, writing in the 1920s, proposed that gifts in traditional societies are never truly free. Instead, they carry an implicit obligation, structured around three linked duties: the obligation to give, the obligation to receive, and, critically, the obligation to reciprocate. Accepting a gift is not a neutral act. It draws the recipient into a relationship, and until something is given back, that relationship remains unbalanced, almost like an unpaid debt. The potlatch ceremonies of Pacific Northwest Coast peoples illustrate this vividly. At a potlatch, a host distributes enormous quantities of food, blankets, and other valuables to guests, sometimes to the point of near self-impoverishment. On the surface, this looks like pure generosity, but Mauss argued it was actually a display and negotiation of status. Guests who received lavish gifts incurred an obligation to host an equally, or more, generous potlatch in the future, or risk losing social standing. Reciprocity, in other words, wasn't calculated in the moment of giving; it unfolded across years, sometimes across generations. What makes this framework compelling to anthropologists today is how widely it applies. Wedding gifts, holiday exchanges, even business favors follow a similar, if less dramatic, logic of obligation and return. The specific customs vary enormously across cultures, but the underlying pattern, that giving creates a bond which reciprocation eventually settles or renews, appears again and again. Mauss's insight was that exchange, far from being purely economic, is fundamentally about weaving and maintaining social relationships.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The specific customs of potlatch ceremonies among Pacific Northwest peoples
options.B: How Marcel Mauss became an influential figure in French sociology
options.C: Why wedding and holiday gifts are more meaningful than business favors
options.D: How gift-giving obligations create and reinforce social bonds across cultures

questions.explanation: 正确答案 D 概括主旨：赠礼所产生的义务如何在不同文化中建立并强化社会纽带。A 只是讲座中用来说明观点的一个具体案例，属 too_narrow。B 谈莫斯本人的学术生涯，讲座并未讲述这一点，属 wrong_topic。C 说婚礼和节日礼物比商业人情更有意义，讲座只说它们遵循相似逻辑，并未做此比较，属 surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what are the three obligations Mauss identified?

options.A: To give, to receive, and to reciprocate
options.B: To give, to refuse, and to redistribute
options.C: To host, to attend, and to donate
options.D: To reciprocate before giving and receiving occur

questions.explanation: 正确答案 A 与讲座一致：给予的义务、接受的义务，以及回报的义务。B 提到“拒绝”，讲座未提及此项义务，属 wrong_detail。C 提到“主持、出席、捐赠”，是与礼物相关但讲座未如此表述的话题，属 topic_adjacent。D 说回报义务发生在给予和接受之前，与讲座描述的顺序相反，属 reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happened to guests who received lavish gifts at a potlatch?

options.A: They were immediately expected to return an equal gift on the spot
options.B: They took on an obligation to host an equally generous potlatch later
options.C: They lost social standing no matter what they did afterward
options.D: They redistributed the gifts to lower-status members of their own community

questions.explanation: 正确答案 B 与讲座一致：客人因接受厚礼而承担了日后举办同等或更盛大夸富宴的义务。A 说需要当场立即回礼，与讲座所述“回报延续数年甚至数代”相反，属 reversed_logic。C 说无论如何都会失去地位，讲座说的是“若不回报才会失去地位”，属 wrong_detail。D 说客人将礼物再分配给本社区地位较低的成员，讲座未提及，属 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about Mauss's framework?

options.A: It suggests that every gift creates a permanent, unbreakable social debt
options.B: It suggests that exchange is fundamentally economic rather than social
options.C: It suggests that modern exchange is still fundamentally about social relationships
options.D: It suggests that gift-giving customs have remained unchanged since Mauss's era

questions.explanation: 正确答案 C 与讲座结尾呼应：交换的本质在于编织和维系社会关系，这一点在现代情境（婚礼、节日、商业人情）中依然适用。A 说每份礼物都造成永久、无法打破的债务，讲座并未如此绝对表述，属 over_generalization。B 说交换本质上是经济行为而非社会行为，与讲座结论“远非纯粹经济行为”正相反，属 reversed_logic。D 说赠礼习俗自莫斯时代以来从未改变，讲座只说具体习俗因文化而异，并未讨论历时变化，属 topic_adjacent。
